items.mistake: Adopting GitFlow at a startup with daily releases.
items.fix: Use trunk-based or GitHub Flow until you actually need parallel versioned releases.
items.why: GitFlow's ceremony (develop/release/hotfix branches) is overhead with no payoff when you only have one live version.
items.example: For most SaaS teams: `main` + feature branches + PRs + CD. That's it.

items.fix: Hide unfinished work behind a flag (`if cfg.experimental.foo:`) instead of long-lived branches.
items.why: Without flags, anyone merging an unfinished feature breaks `main` for the rest of the team.

items.fix: Pick `<type>/<short-name>` and enforce in CI / branch protection.
items.why: Naming makes branch lists, filters and dashboards usable; without it, you can't tell what's in flight.

items.mistake: Tagging releases as lightweight tags without a message.
items.fix: `git tag -a vX.Y.Z -m "..."` — annotated tags carry author, date and message.
items.why: Lightweight tags are just bookmarks; annotated tags carry the release-notes context.
items.example:
```bash
git tag -a v1.2.0 -m "Release 1.2.0: faster reranker, fixed 429 retry"
```

items.mistake: Forgetting to back-merge a GitFlow hotfix to `develop`.
items.fix: After merging the hotfix to `main`, immediately `git merge --no-ff main` into `develop`.
items.why: Without back-merge, the next release inherits a `main` *without* the hotfix — silently reintroducing the bug.
items.example:
```bash
git switch develop && git merge --no-ff main && git push
```
